And you're not a lot in fact one of your clients Matt Gilligley and Tim what's Tim's name? They started as coaches for other coaches who didn't want to do coaching and build a coaching program So all sorts of ways to skin skin this cat So quickly where do we find you? Militia comm okay, I'll tee ee are okay Thank you. Thank you next strategy opposition um Some years ago sort of a foster type story guy comes to one of my seminars for speakers in fact, and well, yeah, and his name is Jack Henry and Jack was then speaking for 500 bucks a day and he was booked solid And he had one product six audio cassette tapes with a rubber band around it that he sold for 49 bucks And so Jack tells me his story at the seminar. We like to hot seats And as I hear Jack's story Important word story I'm like And the fact that he's working for 500 and he's selling six tapes for 49 bucks I'm like thinking this guy should be making a million dollars a minute, and he doesn't know it right because here's Jack's story Jack was Was selling himself as an expert in Employee and delivery man theft control for supermarkets and convenience stores The two minute piece of information you need to fully appreciate this is that The theft by the employees and the delivery men is five times what the shoplifting number is So most of the store owners think their problem is shoplifting the problem is all internal And the delivery guys all steal from them and the employees all steal from them The delivery guys steal what they deliver and they steal other stuff while they're there as well So the cigarette guy comes in with four empty cartons underneath the full cartons And he puts the little cameras in the empty cartons and goes out with cameras The Pepsi guy wheels in six cases of Pepsi stacked on a cart yells over to the guy behind the counter six guy looks out C six checks off six Pepsi guy Wheels back out six empty cartons Two of which are full of beer And two of which are full of Pepsi and only the one on the top is empty And then he goes and sells a beer and a Pepsi for cash to somebody that owns a bar somewhere And so the store owner paid for a delivery of 18 cases of Pepsi and he really got four On and on and on and on the employees. There's 312 ways the employees steal I don't remember them all anymore But I can't walk into any convenience store any grocery store and actually any restaurant in five minutes I can tell you if the cashier is dealing which 90% of the time she is and most of the time I can tell you how much she's stolen up until now on her shift There's things called theft tracks you can do it visually One quick one just for fun you pay attention to if they're stealing at the register From the store not from the customers by short-changing, but if they're stealing from the register They have to wait to get it out when there's not somebody around they got to put the money in a register Which means they got to get it out so they got to keep track of how much over they are in the register They can't do it mentally So they have to have a mechanism for keeping track of how much over they are in the register The most common is a little pile of objects to which they've assigned a value so over here is two pieces of gum a button Three pennies and a bobby pin well each one of those things means some The bobby pins a quarter the buttons is a dollar piece of gum is a nickel and That's how they can add up how far over they are in a register and how much they can take out And as soon as in one chain as soon as you catch one of them They've all taught each other the same numerical code so as soon as you know what the one is doing you can know what they're all doing In grocery stores they put it they tape a barcode It's why if you run a grocery store all short sleeves only because if you let them wear long sleeves They tape a barcode for a cheap item on their wrist and then when their mother-in-law comes through with steak Because the front-end supervisor can only hear the beeps they're listening for beeps They run the steak under the register like that under the thing like this under the scanner like this They scan the $4 barcode and they put the $20 item through the deal on and on and on and on None of that's really here, but I'm just as background so Jack story is he's an ex delivery man thief That's his expertise He was a Pepsi driver and a bread driver and stole From everybody he delivered to And all the delivery guys planet by the way they all arrive at the same time So there's chaos and they all meet up and this one gives what they stole to this one They trade they swap they open a store in a garage together So I'm here in the story right and Jack's telling stories He's got four or five really good stories of theft incidents and He's booked solid at 500 bucks a day. I'm thinking no wonder right so average grocery store loses $500 a day to internal theft Jack's going in solving their whole problem for 500 bucks once and 49 bucks for a set of tape sleep Afterwards, I'll take a jack aside Out of the entire crop that was at the seminar and I'm saying Jack Let's fix this So here's how valuable this information was we immediately raised his fee from 500 to 5,000 By the way, I'm not real subtle about that. I usually just add zeros and see what we wind up with He loses one client Now they all pissed and moaned like there's no tomorrow, but they all paid it Then we took the tapes 490 bucks at a zero no difference same six tapes same rubber band same number of people buying them And then we put in a 15,000 then we took the seminar he was doing cut in half because he was teaching too much So we created basic in advance. So really if you want me to do both, it's not 5,000 It's 10,000 so we really took the fee from 5,000 into 10,000 on and on and on and on all because there was a great story and a And a enemy a thing that irritated the owners They were really mad when you proved to them that they're being ripped off by their employees And by their delivery guys So you got an enemy You got somebody to sell against is the enemy of this owner
